1900s.title: Gold standard price anchors
1900s.body: Many economies still operated around gold-linked monetary systems. Price levels often moved slowly, but the experience varied by country and by data source.

1910s.title: War finance and price pressure
1910s.body: World War I disrupted trade, public finance, and production. Consumer prices rose sharply in many countries as governments borrowed, rationed, and redirected output.

1920s.title: A difficult return to stability
1920s.body: The 1920s mixed postwar adjustment, debt burdens, uneven growth, and attempts to restore monetary order. Purchasing power could move very differently across countries.

1930s.title: Depression-era deflation risk
1930s.body: The Great Depression pushed demand and prices down in many economies. Deflation could increase the purchasing power of cash while economic hardship intensified.

1940s.title: War controls and shortages
1940s.body: World War II brought rationing, shortages, and price controls. Official price index data may understate the lived cost of scarcity in wartime economies.

1950s.title: Postwar rebuilding
1950s.body: Postwar recovery, industrial expansion, and rising household consumption shaped price trends. Inflation was often steadier than during the war years.

1960s.title: Growth before the inflation shock
1960s.body: Strong growth and changing policy regimes defined the decade. In many countries, later-1960s price pressure set the stage for more volatile 1970s inflation.

1970s.title: Oil shocks and monetary change
1970s.body: Energy shocks, exchange-rate changes, and policy uncertainty made the 1970s a turning point for purchasing power in many advanced economies.

1980s.title: Disinflation and high rates
1980s.body: Many central banks fought inflation with tighter policy. The transition often reduced inflation but came with recessions, unemployment, and slower demand.

1990s.title: Lower inflation period
1990s.body: Global trade, technology, and inflation-focused policy helped many economies experience lower and more stable consumer price growth than in the 1970s.

2000s.title: Globalization and financial stress
2000s.body: The decade combined global supply integration with a severe financial crisis. Price trends were shaped by energy, credit conditions, and central bank response.

2010s.title: Low-rate recovery
2010s.body: After the financial crisis, many economies saw low interest rates and modest consumer price growth. Asset prices and consumer prices did not always move together.

2020s.title: Pandemic-era price shock
2020s.body: Supply disruptions, policy support, energy swings, and reopening demand created unusually fast price increases in the early 2020s.
